symptoms.symptom_name: Intense scalp pruritus, worse at night, with sleep disruption

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nocturnal scalp itch is the cardinal symptom of head lice but is non-specific alone — also seen in dermatitis and tinea.
Scalp itch is common to both; does not discriminate on its own.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sensation of crawling/movement in the hair (caregiver-observed movement)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A crawling/tickling sensation with caregiver-observed movement in the hair is highly suggestive of live lice and is not a feature of dermatitis or tinea.
Dandruff does not move; a movement sensation points away from a purely inflammatory scalp condition.

symptoms.symptom_name: Firmly adherent greyish-white nits within 1 cm of the scalp, clustered behind the ears and occiput

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nits cemented to hair shafts near the scalp that do not slide or flick off, at the classic retroauricular/occipital sites, are highly specific for active or recent infestation. Caregiver can partially observe this on video; definitive confirmation is by examination/dermoscopy.
Dandruff flakes are loose and easily removed; firmly adherent specks effectively exclude simple dandruff.

symptoms.symptom_name: Live crawling lice visualised on the scalp

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Direct visualisation of a live louse is diagnostic. In the source this was a clinician finding on close inspection; in telehealth it may be partially seen on caregiver video but cannot be relied upon for definitive confirmation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Excoriations across the scalp and posterior neck from scratching

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Excoriation reflects chronic scratching from the pruritus; supports an itchy scalp condition and marks the site at risk of secondary infection.
Broken skin is the portal of entry — the reason to safety-net for impetiginisation/cellulitis.
Reactive posterior cervical/occipital nodes are a common accompaniment of an inflamed, excoriated scalp; non-specific but consistent.
Tender regional nodes can accompany early skin infection; a marked, hot, fluctuant node would escalate concern.

symptoms.symptom_name: No honey-coloured crusting, pus, or spreading erythema currently

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of honey-coloured crusting and spreading redness argues against current impetiginisation/cellulitis, keeping the case at baseline (benign) tier.

symptoms.symptom_name: No circular patches of hair loss, broken 'black-dot' hairs, or boggy kerion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The absence of scaly alopecic patches, black-dot hairs, or a kerion makes tinea capitis unlikely.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever or systemic illness; child alert and feeding

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No fever or systemic upset makes significant secondary infection unlikely at present and supports a low baseline tier.

symptoms.symptom_name: No relief from over-the-counter anti-dandruff shampoo

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Failure of an anti-dandruff shampoo makes seborrhoeic dermatitis less likely and is consistent with an infestation that needs a pediculicide, not an antifungal shampoo.

symptoms.symptom_name: Shared bedroom/bedding with siblings — transmission risk factor

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Close head-to-head contact and shared sleeping arrangements are the main transmission routes and drive the need to check/treat household contacts.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Nocturnal scalp itch + crawling sensation + firmly adherent nits near the scalp at retroauricular/occipital sites + visualised lice + excoriation together make active head lice a confident, straightforward diagnosis and exclude dandruff.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Wet-combing detection with a fine-tooth lice comb and conditioner
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Conditioner-and-comb detection is the practical, at-home way to confirm live lice and is also the mechanical treatment; the caregiver can do it and report findings.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Close-up photo / video of the scalp behind the ears and occiput
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Supports remote confirmation of adherent nits versus dandruff flakes when direct examination is not possible in telehealth.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Dermoscopy or microscopy of a plucked hair
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Reserved for genuinely borderline cases in an in-person setting; direct visualisation of lice/nits is sufficient for management and this should not be routinely ordered.
No role in uncomplicated head lice. A swab is only relevant if secondary bacterial infection develops; ordering bloods here is over-investigation.
